威胁是舒适地转向不理解你在做什么

The threat is comfortable drift toward not understanding what you're doing

ergosphere.blog 作者 zaikunzhang 分数 865 评论 576 2026/04/05 17:57
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

暂无内容

评论（默认中文）

Wowfunhappy 2026/04/05 21:55
> 施瓦茨的实验是最具启发性的，但其原因却并非他所认为的那样。他证明了克劳德可以在详细的监督下写出一篇技术严谨的物理论文。如果你仔细阅读的话，他实际上证明了监督就是物理学。克劳德在三天内完成了完整的初稿。看起来很专业。方程看起来是正确的。情节符合预期。然后施瓦茨读了它，发现它是错误的。克劳德一直在调整参数以使图匹配，而不是发现实际错误。它伪造了结果。它发明了系数。 [...] 施瓦茨发现了这一切，因为他几十年来一直在研究理论物理学。他知道答案应该是什么样子。他知道需要进行哪些交叉检查。 [...] 如果施瓦茨是鲍勃而不是施瓦茨，那么这篇论文就会是错误的，而且他们都不会知道。

因此，矛盾的是，法学硕士只有在您是施瓦茨的情况下才有用†，并且您不能通过使用法学硕士来成为施瓦茨。

这意味着我们 需要 像爱丽丝这样的人！我们必须 腾出空间 对于像爱丽丝这样的人，找到一种方法将她提升到鲍勃之上，尽管鲍勃可能看起来更快。

这篇文章指出了这一点，但我认为它还不够明确。看起来不太实用。但我们必须找到一种方法，否则当下一代不知道如何评估法学硕士的成果时，我们都会陷入深深的麻烦！

---

† 在这种情况下，“有用”的意思是“帮助您产生有益于人类的良好科学”。

> Schwartz's experiment is the most revealing, and not for the reason he thinks. What he demonstrated is that Claude can, with detailed supervision, produce a technically rigorous physics paper. What he actually demonstrated, if you read carefully, is that the supervision is the physics. Claude produced a complete first draft in three days. It looked professional. The equations seemed right. The plots matched expectations. Then Schwartz read it, and it was wrong. Claude had been adjusting parameters to make plots match instead of finding actual errors. It faked results. It invented coefficients. [...] Schwartz caught all of this because he's been doing theoretical physics for decades. He knew what the answer should look like. He knew which cross-checks to demand. [...] If Schwartz had been Bob instead of Schwartz, the paper would have been wrong, and neither of them would have known.

And so the paradox is, the LLMs are only useful† if you're Schwartz, and you can't become Schwartz by using LLMs.

Which means we need people like Alice! We have to make space for people like Alice, and find a way to promote her over Bob, even though Bob may seem to be faster.

The article gestures at this but I don't think it comes down hard enough. It doesn't seem practical. But we have to find a way, or we're all going to be in deep trouble when the next generation doesn't know how to evaluate what the LLMs produce!

---

† "Useful" in this context means "helps you produce good science that benefits humanity".

conception 2026/04/05 22:47
遗憾的是，我不明白我们当前的社会范式是如何实现这一点的。历史上没有任何像这样的长期计划或对雇员和雇主的这种熟练工行会式培训的长期忠诚（无论方向）。人工智能高管基本上都在竞赛，希望在他们全部消失之前我们不需要施瓦茨。但是，对于大学毕业生有什么激励措施，让他们在没有法学硕士的情况下工作十年，然后为他们提供加速工作的工具呢？
Sadly I don’t see how our current social paradigm works for this. There is no history of any sort of long planning like this or long term loyalty (either direction) with employees and employers for this sort of journeyman guild style training. AI execs are basically racing, hoping we won’t need a Schwartz before they are all gone. But what incentives are in place to high a college grad, have them work without llms for a decade and then give them the tools to accelerate their work?

Wowfunhappy 2026/04/05 23:01
那么社会范式需要改变。当人工智能摧毁学术界（可能还有更多）时，每个人都会翻身而死吗？

去年 9 月，泰勒·奥斯汀·哈珀 (Tyler Austin Harper) 在《大西洋月刊》上发表了一篇文章，阐述他认为大学应该如何应对人工智能。他的建议是 激进的——但是，如果你得出结论认为人工智能确实会摧毁这些机构所代表的一切，我认为你至少必须考虑这些措施。 https://www.theatlantic.com/culture/archive/2025/09/ai-colle...

Then the social paradigm needs to change. Is everyone just going to roll over and die while AI destroys academia (and possibly a lot more)?

Last September, Tyler Austin Harper published a piece for The Atlantic on how he thinks colleges should respond to AI. What he proposes is radical—but, if you've concluded that AI really is going to destroy everything these institutions stand for, I think you have to at least consider these sorts of measures. https://www.theatlantic.com/culture/archive/2025/09/ai-colle...

在 HN 中查看
pxc 2026/04/06 02:08
在我到达这一部分之前我非常感兴趣：

> 无例外政策很重要的另一个原因是：如果允许残疾学生使用笔记本电脑和人工智能，那么很大一部分其他学生很可能会找到获得相同津贴的方法，从而使禁令变得毫无用处。当我还是一名教授时，我一次又一次地目睹了这一点——没有残疾的学生为了自己的利益而想方设法利用残疾便利设施。我认识的仍在课堂上的教授告诉我，这仍然是一个严重的问题。

对于患有严重且无法矫正的视力障碍的学生来说，这将是一个巨大的问题。患有退行性眼病的人已经不得不一遍又一遍地重新学习如何做生活中的每一件事。今天对他们有效的方法将不可避免地失败，他们必须重新开始。

但像这样的身体缺陷也很难伪造，而且很容易准确识别。事实上，许多大学的残疾服务只为你提供与你的实际情况有关的住宿。

还有一些事情如果没有技术就很难适应。例如，我姐姐身体上无法阅读报纸。纸张无法达到适合她的对比度。有时，她唯一能读到的东西就是深色模式下的 OLED 屏幕，背景完全是黑色的；她需要极高的对比度。她不懂盲文（现在大多数盲人都不懂），因为她小时候并不是盲人。

忠诚的作弊者无论如何都会作弊；当代人工智能非常擅长 OCR。通过这样的政策，你可以成功地惩罚诚实的残疾人，但你无法阻止严重的作弊者。

I was pretty interested until I got to this part:

> Another reason that a no-exceptions policy is important: If students with disabilities are permitted to use laptops and AI, a significant percentage of other students will most likely find a way to get the same allowances, rendering the ban useless. I witnessed this time and again when I was a professor—students without disabilities finding ways to use disability accommodations for their own benefit. Professors I know who are still in the classroom have told me that this remains a serious problem.

This would be a huge problem for students with severe and uncorrectable visual impairments. People with degenerative eye diseases already have to relearn how to do every single thing in their life over and over and over. What works for them today will inevitably fail, and they have to start over.

But physical impairments like this are also difficult to fake and easy to discern accurately. It's already the case that disability services at many universities only grants you accommodations that have something to do with your actual condition.

There are also some things that are just difficult to accommodate without technology. For instance, my sister physically cannot read paper. Paper is not capable of contrast ratios that work for her. The only things she can even sometimes read are OLED screens in dark mode, with absolutely black backgrounds; she requires an extremely high contrast ratio. She doesn't know braille (which most blind people don't, these days) because she was not blind as a little girl.

Committed cheaters will be able to cheat anyway; contemporary AI is great at OCR. You'll successfully punish honest disabled people with a policy like this but you won't stop serious cheaters.

greazy 2026/04/06 09:12
作者并没有直接建议禁止所有技术。他们甚至连接到数字打字机。在您引用的这段话之后，他们建议提供一种更加以人为本的方法来帮助残疾人。建议你的妹妹可以继续学习上述两种解决方案，这并不是一个巨大的飞跃；结合 OLED 屏幕的残疾导师。
The author did not outright suggest the banning of all technology. They even linked to a digital typewriter. After the very paragraph you quote, they suggest instead to offer a more human centric approach to helping disabled people. It's not a huge leap to suggest that your sister could continue to learn with the above two solutions; a disability tutor combined with a OLED screen.

irishcoffee 2026/04/06 08:36
我不知道这里其他人的情况，但大学并没有教育意义，因为我正在上大学。我自己完成了所有的阅读和学习。课程不是很有趣，我的大多数助教根本不会说母语，一半的教授也一样。

我很享受我的时光，结交了很多终生的朋友，并弄清楚了如何独自生活。我的朋友参加了新兵训练营而不是大学，免费学习了所有这些相同的技能。

教育不会毁掉或玷污我的法学硕士，整件事一开始就是个笑话。毁掉大学的是无限制的学生贷款……而我们所有管理大学的最优秀、最聪明的人都为了钱而强奸学生。这是可悲的、邪恶的，而且不知何故受到拥护。

I don’t know about anyone else here, but college was not educating because I was at college. I did all of the reading and studying on my own. The classes weren’t very interesting, most of my TAs didn’t speak the native language well at all, nor did half the professors.

I enjoyed my time, I made a lot of lifelong friends, and figured out how to live on my own. My buddies that enrolled in boot camp instead of college learned all those same skills, for free.

Education won’t be ruined or blemished my LLMs, the whole thing was a joke to begin with. The bit that ruined college was unlimited student loans… and all of our best and brightest folks running the colleges raping students for money. It’s pathetic, evil, and somehow espoused.

在 HN 中查看
bsder 2026/04/06 07:31
他指的是非常优秀的学生，他们的父母会去购买医疗诊断，以便他们的孩子可以获得“住宿”，例如额外的时间来完成考试。

问题是这是治标不治本。 其症状是大学入学和成绩作弊过于有效。 原因是大学录取和成绩已经成为高风险，这是绝对不应该的。

What he is referring to are perfectly good students whose parents will go shopping for a medical diagnosis so that their child can get "accommodation" like extra time to complete tests.

The problem is that this is treating the symptom rather than the cause. The symptom is that cheating for college admission and achievement is too effective. The cause is that college admission and achievement has become high stakes, and it absolutely should not be.

foobarchu 2026/04/06 02:55
I was there as well, the bush presidency lasted my entire middle and high school career, and I got the chance to vote for Obama in my senior year.

I remember things very differently. Everyone cared about the Iraq war, gay-straight alliance was one of the most up and coming clubs, and political music was everywhere. Green Day had their big second wave with American Idiot, System Of A Down was on top of the world, Rock Against Bush was huge, anarcho-punk like Rise Against was getting big.

I'm not a teenager anymore obviously, so it's entirely possible I'm just missing it, but I've seen very little that compares to those sort of movements. On the other hand, most millennials I know are still wildly politically active.

bandofthehawk 2026/04/06 04:52
In 2002, there war in Iraq had large popular support, something like 70-80 percent. It took a few years for people to realize it was based on a lie and was a massive mistake. It was also morally reprehensible, but that part is not often discussed in mainstream US politics.

Wowfunhappy 2026/04/06 00:32
> 在我最近任教的地方——一所小型的、选择性的、私立文理学院——如果管理者有勇气的话，他们可以在限制人工智能的使用方面走得很远。他们不仅应该致力于对教室、而且对整个机构进行无情的去科技化。摆脱 Wi-Fi 并回归以太网，这将使学校能够更好地控制学生使用数字技术的时间和地点。为此，校园内也应禁止使用智能手机和笔记本电脑。如果学生想在课堂上打笔记或在图书馆打论文，他们可以使用数字打字机，它只有文字处理功能，但没有其他功能。需要学生使用互联网或计算机的工作和研究可以在指定的实验室进行。 [...] 特别致力于维持这种无技术环境的大学可能会要求学生住在校园里，这样他们就无法在家中使用人工智能工具而不被发现。

您可以访问完整的文章： https://archive.is/zSJ13 （我知道 archive.is 有点阴暗，但它有效）。

> At the type of place where I taught until recently—a small, selective, private liberal-arts college—administrators can go quite far in limiting AI use, if they have the guts to do so. They should commit to a ruthless de-teching not just of classrooms but of their entire institution. Get rid of Wi-Fi and return to Ethernet, which would allow schools greater control over where and when students use digital technologies. To that end, smartphones and laptops should also be banned on campus. If students want to type notes in class or papers in the library, they can use digital typewriters, which have word processing but nothing else. Work and research requiring students to use the internet or a computer can take place in designated labs. [...] Colleges that are especially committed to maintaining this tech-free environment could require students to live on campus, so they can’t use AI tools at home undetected.

You can access the full article at https://archive.is/zSJ13 (I know archive.is is kind of shady, but it works).

boothby 2026/04/06 01:18
> 如果学生想在课堂上打笔记或在图书馆打论文，他们可以使用数字打字机，它具有文字处理功能，但没有其他功能。

只是，在今天，更换这样一台机器的内部结构来容纳本地法学硕士是非常容易的。 目前，为设备供电所需的电池质量将是一个赠品，但推理正在变得越来越便宜。

> 特别致力于维持这种无技术环境的大学可能会要求学生住在校园里，这样他们就无法在家中使用人工智能工具而不被发现。

我确信，就像我的校园同学从不吸食大麻或饮酒的未成年人一样。

> If students want to type notes in class or papers in the library, they can use digital typewriters, which have word processing but nothing else.

Only, replacing the guts of such a machine to contain a local LLM is damn easy today. Right now the battery mass required to power the device would be a giveaway, but inference is getting energetically cheaper.

> Colleges that are especially committed to maintaining this tech-free environment could require students to live on campus, so they can’t use AI tools at home undetected.

Just like my on-campus classmates never smoked weed or drank underage, I'm sure.

在 HN 中查看
ninalanyon 2026/04/06 15:54
Just return to old fashioned styles of universities with tutorials, lectures, offline handwritten exams, and viva voce.

It's very hard to hide the fact that someone else did an assignment when you have to defend it in front your tutor and a small group of fellow students and it's next to impossible to pass a final viva without knowing and understanding what you are talking about.

The problem is we have all become addicted to cheap 'education' and a the traditional methods are expensive.

But I think the institutions and the students need to ask themselves what the university is for. Is it to hand out diplomas or is it there so that the students can learn? A student who only wants the diploma has an incentive to cheat, one who wants to learn does not because the only person cheated is themself.

Just return to old fashioned styles of universities with tutorials, lectures, offline handwritten exams, and viva voce.

It's very hard to hide the fact that someone else did an assignment when you have to defend it in front your tutor and a small group of fellow students and it's next to impossible to pass a final viva without knowing and understanding what you are talking about.

The problem is we have all become addicted to cheap 'education' and a the traditional methods are expensive.

But I think the institutions and the students need to ask themselves what the university is for. Is it to hand out diplomas or is it there so that the students can learn? A student who only wants the diploma has an incentive to cheat, one who wants to learn does not because the only person cheated is themself.

senordevnyc 2026/04/06 04:28
这不是针对你的，但在我看来，这正是学术界一直受到嘲笑的那种脱离现实世界的思维。此类提案将 绝不 如果发生的话，我基本上愿意赌上我的生命。学生（和他们的家长）对这种反技术的废话兴趣为零，所以这是 DOA。大学不是强制性的，这些学生也不是你可以为所欲为的俘虏，他们是 顾客。坦率地说，我怀疑大多数教授或管理人员是否都希望这样。
This isn't aimed at you, but this strikes me as exactly the kind of divorced-from-the-real-world thinking that academia is pilloried for all the time. This kind of proposal will never happen, I'd basically stake my life on it. Students (and their parents) have zero interest in this kind of anti-technology nonsense, so it's DOA. College isn't compulsory, and those students aren't some captive audience you can do whatever you want with, they're customers. And I frankly doubt that most professors or administrators want this either.

joe_the_user 2026/04/06 05:36
嗯，天体物理学的情况很特殊，因为正如文章指出的那样，没有可以外部验证的突破。

其他项目的成功与施瓦茨的数量成正比，因此它们似乎不太可能消失。但对于那些没有直接资金的地区，它们可能会消失。

Well, the astrophysics situation is special because, as the article notes, there aren't breakthroughs that can be externally verified.

Other projects' success will be proportional to their number of Schwartz' and so it seems unlikely they disappear. But they may disappear for areas in which there is no immediate money.

在 HN 中查看
cmiles74 2026/04/05 22:51
我想我们已经知道我们需要做什么：鼓励人们自己做工作，阻止初学者立即向法学硕士寻求帮助并重新引入某种口语考试。正如文章提到的，禁止法学硕士是不切实际的，我们真正需要的是那些能够辨别法学硕士何时有把握错误的人；不是那些不知道如何与法学硕士一起工作的人。

我希望它能鼓励人们更多地思考他们从工作中得到什么，工作对他们有什么好处；我认为这是一件好事。

I think we already know what we need to do: encourage people to do the work themselves, discourage beginners from immediately asking an LLM for help and re-introducing some kind of oral exam. As the article mentions, banning LLMs is impractical and what we really need are people who can tell when the LLM is confidently wrong; not people who don't know how to work with an LLM.

I hope it will encourage people to think more about what they get out of the work, what doing the work does for them; I think that's a good thing.

在 HN 中查看
atomicnumber3 2026/04/05 23:58
我想我们会到达那里。不过，我们至少需要先让人工智能破产。对于那些认为人工智能将完全取代工程师的人，甚至那些认为虽然它可能不会取代工程师，但它将在一年内完成 100% 的编码的人来说，这是不可能的。或者甚至它现在可以完成 100% 的编码。

有几个不幸的事实同时发生：

- 有钱的人正在尝试建立“完美”的业务：没有软件工程人员的 SaaS。 100% 保证金。 0 资本支出。最后，运营支出和研发成本接近于 0。或者至少，他们试图向任何愿意购买的人推销这个想法。不幸的是，这正是大多数投资者想听到的，因此他们相信每句话并投入大量资金。当然，这会扩展到许多其他业务，而不仅仅是 SaaS，但这些业务一开始的利润率就较差，因此不太容易发生野火。

- 15 年前曾经编写代码但现在不编写代码的人会看到 claude 生成了看起来非常合理的代码。鉴于他们现在的工作是“首席高管”或“总监”，他们不会察觉到任何直接的个人风险，因此嗅觉测试通过了，他们都在船上，愉快地一路上造成破坏。

- 名义上是软件工程师但不擅长软件的人确实被克劳德提升了 100 倍。不幸的是，如果他们的起点接近 0，这并不能说明什么。如果是负数，现在是负数的 100 倍。

- 与软件工程相关的人，比如产品经理，特别是如果他们同时涉足编码，突然也会发现他们现在“可以编码”。

当然，现在并非所有资本所有者、首席技术官、产品经理等都表现出这一点。甚至可能不是大多数。但我已经可以从我认识的人那里说出上面每个类别的 4 个例子。现在它们都无法解释任何细微差别。有太多的人和文章和博客文章告诉他们他们是绝对正确的。

我们需要一些萧条周期。然后，也许我们可以就如何利用法学硕士（我们将不再称其为“人工智能”……）进行富有成效的讨论，以继续进行被称为软件工程的团队运动。

因为这里可以真正提高生产力。不幸的是，他们并没有用 AGI 取代所有人，也没有允许不懂编码或软件工程的人构建实际的工作软件，而且他们也不涉及让克劳德代码随机地为你生成一个初创公司。

I think we'll get there. We need to get at least some AI bust going first though. It's impossible to talk sense into people who think AI is about to completely replace engineers, or even those who think that, while it might not replace engineers, it's going to be doing 100% of all coding within a year. Or even that it can do 100% of coding right now.

There's a couple unfortunate truths going on all at the same time:

- People with money are trying to build the "perfect" business: SaaS without software eng headcount. 100% margin. 0 Capex. And finally near-0 opex and R&D cost. Or at least, they're trying to sell the idea of this to anyone who will buy. And unfortunately this is exactly what most investors want to hear, so they believe every word and throw money at it. This of course then extends to many other business and not just SaaS, but those have worse margins to start with so are less prone to the wildfire.

- People who used to code 15 years ago but don't now, see claude generating very plausible looking code. Given their job is now "C suite" or "director", they don't perceive any direct personal risk, so the smell test is passed and they're all on board, happily wreaking destruction along the way.

- People who are nominally software engineers but are bad at it are truly elevated 100x by claude. Unfortunately, if their starting point was close to 0, this isn't saying a lot. And if it was negative, it's now 100x as negative.

- People who are adjacent to software engineering, like PMs, especially if they dabble in coding on the side, suddenly also see they "can code" now.

Now of course, not all capital owners, CTOs, PMs, etc exhibit this. Probably not even most. But I can already name like 4 example per category above from people I know. And they're all impossible to explain any kind of nuance to right now. There's too many people and articles and blog posts telling them they're absolutely right.

We need some bust cycle. Then maybe we can have a productive discussion of how we can leverage LLMs (we'll stop calling it "AI"...) to still do the team sport known as software engineering.

Because there's real productivity gains to be had here. Unfortunately, they don't replace everyone with AGI or allow people who don't know coding or software engineering to build actual working software, and they don't involve just letting claude code stochastically generate a startup for you.

在 HN 中查看
Wowfunhappy 2026/04/06 00:04
> 甚至 [AI] 现在可以完成 100% 的编码。

我实际上并不认为这篇文章反驳了这一点。但人工智能需要掌握在能够审查代码（或天体物理学论文）、注意到和理解问题并告诉人工智能要进行哪些更改的人手中。冲洗，重复。它可能仍然比自己编写所有代码更快（但这并不意味着您可以解雇所有工程师）。

问题是，你如何成为一个能够有效审查人工智能代码的人，而无需在没有人工智能的情况下实际编写代码？我基本上会争论你 不能。

> Or even that [AI] can do 100% of coding right now.

I don't actually think the article refutes this. But the AI needs to be in the hands of someone who can review the code (or astrophysics paper), notice and understand issues, and tell the AI what changes to make. Rinse, repeat. It's still probably faster than writing all the code yourself (but that doesn't mean you can fire all your engineers).

The question is, how do you become the person who can effectively review AI code without actually writing code without an AI? I'd argue you basically can't.

在 HN 中查看
bluefirebrand 2026/04/06 01:46
> 问题是，你如何成为一个能够有效审查人工智能代码的人，而无需在没有人工智能的情况下实际编写代码？我认为你基本上不能。

我同意，并且我会更进一步：

你可以成为世界上绝对最好的编码员，有史以来最快、最准确的代码审查员，但人工智能产生糟糕代码的速度仍然比你审查的速度快得多，无论发生什么，它最终都会成为一种责任

没有大量的“循环法学硕士”“使用一群代理”或任何其他当前的技巧来解决这个问题，因为最终有些人需要阅读和理解代码。所有这一切。

任何避免阅读和理解代码的尝试都意味着您绝对离开了高质量软件的领域，无一例外

> The question is, how do you become the person who can effectively review AI code without actually writing code without an AI? I'd argue you basically can't.

I agree, and I'd go a step further:

You can be the absolute best coder in the world, the fastest and most accurate code reviewer ever to live, and AI still produces bad code so much faster than you can review that it will become a liability eventually no matter what

There is no amount of "LLM in a loop" "use a swarm of agents" or any other current trickery that fixes this because eventually some human needs to read and understand the code. All of it.

Any attempt to avoid reading and understanding the code means you have absolutely left the realm of quality software, no exceptions

在 HN 中查看
doug_durham 2026/04/06 02:31
这篇文章是一个思想实验。作者假设鲍勃没有获得与爱丽丝相同的好处。这个假设可能是错误的。我不知道，作者也不知道。鲍勃可能会拥有非常成功的职业生涯，并且会深入了解该领域，因为他能够更快地解决更广泛的问题。目前，这还只是假设。我认为我们不能说我们需要更多的爱丽丝，就像我们不能说我们需要更多的鲍勃一样。 不幸的是，我们将不得不拭目以待。学术界将负责加强质量控制。 这可能是值得担心的弱点。
The article is a thought experiment. The author hypothesizes that Bob isn't getting the same benefit that Alice is getting. That hypothesis could be wrong. I don't know and the author doesn't know. It could be that Bob is going to have a very successful career and will deeply know the field because he is able to traverse a wider set of problems more quickly. At this point, it's just hypothesis. I don't think that we can say we need more Alices any more than we can say we need more Bobs. Unfortunately we will have to wait and see. It will be upon the academic community to do the work to enforce quality controls. That is probably the weakness to worry about.
在 HN 中查看
MarkusQ 2026/04/06 03:37
我们确实知道。 人们总是有办法避免痛苦的学习过程，但……他们不学习。

这是一个竞争性的思想实验：

Jorge's Gym 拥有一流的健身计划，其中包括一系列广泛的练习，想要成为健美运动员需要花费数年时间才能完成该计划。 您注册后，巧妙地使用滑轮系统，在几周而不是几年内完成所有练习。

你得到了预期的结果吗？

We do know. There have always been ways that people could avoid the painful process of learning, and...they don't learn.

Here's a competing thought experiment:

Jorge's Gym has a top notch body building program, which includes an extensive series of exercises that would-be body builders need to do over multiple years to complete the program. You enroll, and cleverly use a block and tackle system to complete all the exercises in weeks instead of years.

Did you get the intended results?

bandofthehawk 2026/04/06 06:27
更公平的比较可能是，通过阅读一篇完整的论文与在相似的时间内阅读 3-4 篇论文的摘要、询问有关细节的问题、阅读您感兴趣的论文部分等，您是否能学到同样多的知识？
A more fair comparison might be, do you learn as much by reading one full paper vs. in a similar amount of time reading summaries of 3-4 papers, asking questions about details, reading the portions of those papers that you are interested in, etc.

greazy 2026/04/06 09:19
我不同意上述两个类比。有时您必须深入研究一篇论文，而有时则需要进行广泛的研究。针对不同的任务使用不同的工具和方法。

你在这里描述什么

> 阅读那些论文中您感兴趣的部分

是一种没有人会反对的技术的混合使用。

问题仍然是学生是否有远见，使用正确的方法并取得最佳的学习成果。

就我自己而言，当我在大学时，我绝对会让法学硕士总结大量文本并写论文。但这只是我。也许现在的学习者比我更好。

I don't agree with both of the above analogies. Sometimes you must go in depth on a single paper, while other times it's broad research that's required. Different tools and methods for different tasks.

What your describing here

> reading the portions of those papers that you are interested in

Is a hybrid use of the technology which no one would argue against.

The question remains if students have the foresight to use the correct method and which results in the best learning outcomes.

For myself, I would have absolutely let LLMs summarise swaths of text and write my essays when I was at Uni. That's just me though. Maybe today's learners are better than I.

在 HN 中查看
throw310822 2026/04/06 02:35
> 矛盾的是，法学硕士只有在您是施瓦茨时才有用†，并且您不能通过使用法学硕士来成为施瓦茨。

你不能通过法学硕士“成为施瓦茨”，这是一个未经证实的假设。实际上，这是本文逻辑上的矛盾：如果 Bob 设法通过使用 LLM 产生有效的输出 根本不，那么这意味着他一定已经获得了论文所声称的那种必要的监督能力。

顺便说一句，请注意，在思想实验中，鲍勃不仅仅将所有工作委托给法学硕士。他让它总结文章、提取重要知识并澄清概念。这是学习过程的一部分，而不是成为被动的消费者。

> the paradox is, the LLMs are only useful† if you're Schwartz, and you can't become Schwartz by using LLMs.

That you can't "become Schwartz" by using LLMs is an unproven assumption. Actually, it's a contradiction in the logic of the essay: if Bob managed to produce a valid output by using an LLM at all, then it means that he must have acquired precisely that supervision ability that the essay claims to be necessary.

Btw, note that in the thought experiment Bob isn't just delegating all the work to the LLM. He makes it summarise articles, extract important knowledge and clarify concepts. This is part of a process of learning, not being a passive consumer.

在 HN 中查看
Denvercoder9 2026/04/06 03:42
这并不矛盾，关键是鲍勃能够使用法学硕士产生有效的输出，但前提是他自己受到监督；并且他没有培养将来独立监督自己的技能。
There's no contradiction, the point is that Bob is able to produce valid output using LLMs, but only while he himself is being supervised; and that he doesn't develop the skills to supervise independently himself in the future.
在 HN 中查看
throw310822 2026/04/06 04:00
> 仅当他本人受到监督时

不，这是不可能的，除非鲍勃在每周的会议上简单地展示法学硕士的成果并将导师的反馈直接输入其中。每周总共工作 10 分钟，导师至少会立即注意到缺乏进展。

不，文章指出鲍勃实际上 与 法学硕士不只是委托。他要求代理进行总结、解释并帮助修复错误。你可以很容易地认为，鲍勃拥有这样一位 24/7 全天候可用的人工智能导师，可以更快地发展理解能力。他当然不会把时间浪费在 python 语法的小细节上（尽管与“编码专家”合作将使他的代码更加干净和先进）。

> only while he himself is being supervised

No, this is impossible unless Bob is presenting at each weekly meeting simply the output of the LLM and feeding the tutor's feedback straight into it. For a total of 10 minutes work per week, and the tutor would notice straight away at least for the lack of progress.

No, the article specifies that Bob actually works with the LLM, doesn't just delegate. He asks the agent to summarise, to explain, and to help with bug fixing. You could easily argue that Bob, having such an AI tutor available 24/7, can develop understanding much faster. He certainly won't waste his time with small details of python syntax (though working with a "coding expert" will make his code much cleaner and more advanced).

在 HN 中查看
drawnwren 2026/04/06 06:38
这就是问题所在，如果 Bob 始终提供不可靠的 LLM 输出，他就不会得到晋升。为了获得晋升，鲍勃需要学习从法学硕士获得可靠成果的技能。这些可能与爱丽丝学到的技能不同，但如果论点是施瓦茨的法学硕士输出很有价值——为什么我们要假设鲍勃的道路不是通向施瓦茨的呢？
This is the rub, Bob would not be promoted if he consistently provided unreliable LLM output. In order to get promoted, Bob needs to learn the skills that get reliable output out of an LLM. These may not be the same skills that Alice learns, but if the argument is that Schwartz's LLM output is valuable -- why are we to assume Bob's path isn't towards Schwartz?
在 HN 中查看
MarkusQ 2026/04/06 03:31
这并不违背文章的逻辑。

有些花看起来和闻起来都像雌性黄蜂，足以欺骗雄性黄蜂与它们“交配”。 但之后它们不会飞走并产下黄蜂卵。

It doesn't contradict the logic of the essay.

There are flowers that look & smell like female wasps well enough to fool male wasps into "mating" with them. But they don't fly off and lay wasp eggs afterwards.

在 HN 中查看
kmijyiyxfbklao 2026/04/06 07:08
但我们可以区分花朵和黄蜂。如果我们无法区分施瓦茨和非施瓦茨，那么我们很容易受到有或没有人工智能的样本问题的影响。如果存在区别，那么我们可以使用该区别来测试鲍勃，并让他从测试失败中吸取教训。
But there is a distinction we can make between flowers and wasps. If there is no distinction we can make between Schwartz and non-Schwartz, then we are susceptible to the sample problem with or without AI. And if there is a distinction then we can use that distinction to test Bob, and make him learn from his test failures.

mojuba 2026/04/06 02:09
> 这意味着我们需要像爱丽丝这样的人！我们必须为像爱丽丝这样的人腾出空间，并找到一种方法来提升她而不是鲍勃

不过，解决方案相对简单 - 不确定文章是否建议这样做，因为我只是浏览了一下：

在你的领域表现出色不仅意味着推送文章，还意味着能够谈论它们。我认为学术界应该从书面形式转向更多的口头形式，即会议。

比如说，如果你只能在亲自展示你的作品、回答问题等之后才能发表一些东西怎么办？观众可以大也可以小，这并不重要。

这会让出版任何东西变得更加昂贵，但也许这正是学术界所需要的，即使不考虑人工智能的热潮？

> Which means we need people like Alice! We have to make space for people like Alice, and find a way to promote her over Bob

The solution is relatively simple though - not sure the article suggests this as I only skimmed through:

Being good in your field doesn't only mean pushing articles but also being able to talk about them. I think academia should drift away from written form toward more spoken form, i.e. conferences.

What if, say, you can only publish something after presenting your work in person, answer questions, etc? The audience can be big or small, doesn't matter.

It would make publishing anything at all more expensive but maybe that's exactly what academia needs even irrespective of this AI craze?

在 HN 中查看
fomoz 2026/04/06 00:25
人工智能是技能的促进剂，而不是替代品。至少现在还没有。

我用 Python+typescript 构建了一个完整的堆栈应用程序，其中人工智能代理每天处理 10k+ 近乎实时的决策和执行。

我从未做过全栈开发，如果没有 GitHub Copilot，我就无法做到这一点，但我在 IT（数据）领域工作了 15 年，其中 6 年担任领导职务。我从头开始建立了许多系统和团队，建立了流程以确保准确性并最大限度地减少错误等等。

通过向编码代理询问有关应用程序的问题、从中汲取想法、共同规划等等，我学到了大量有关全栈开发的知识。

所以，是的，如果你想构建比廉价的一次性项目更大的东西，那么你需要知道你在做什么，这种项目可行，但没有带来任何价值，而且没有人真正会使用。

现在就是这样，但与此同时，自 2022 年以来，人工智能编码代理已经取得了令人难以置信的长足进步！我确实认为它们会改进，但它无法确切知道你想要构建什么。它正在做出有根据的猜测。您要求它执行的操作的近似值。你问同一件事两次，它会得到两个略有不同的结果（假设这是一个大的一击）。

这是法学硕士的基本现实，有点像人类步行（人工智能之前的情况）、人类使用汽车到达目的地（我们现在的情况）和 FSD（这是未来，看看与第一辆汽车相比需要多长时间）。

AI is an accelerant, not a replacement for skill. At least, not yet.

I built a full stack app in Python+typescript where AI agents process 10k+ near-real-time decisions and executions per day.

I have never done full stack development and I would not have been able to do it without GitHub Copilot, but I have worked in IT (data) for 15 years including 6 in leadership. I have built many systems and teams from scratch, set up processes to ensure accuracy and minimize mistakes, and so on.

I have learned a ton about full stack development by asking the coding agent questions about the app, bouncing ideas off of it, planning together, and so on.

So yes, you need to have an idea of what you're doing if you want to build anything bigger than a cheap one shot throwaway project that sort of works, but brings no value and nobody is actually gonna use.

This is how it is right now, but at the same time AI coding agents have come an incredibly long way since 2022! I do think they will improve but it can't exactly know what you want to build. It's making an educated guess. An approximation of what you're asking it to do. You ask the same thing twice and it will have two slightly different results (assuming it's a big one shot).

This is the fundamental reality of LLMs, sort of like having a human walking (where we were before AI), a human using a car to get to places (where we are now) and FSD (this is future, look how long this took compared to the first cars).

在 HN 中查看
vinceguidry 2026/04/06 02:50
我一直在使用 ChatGPT 来重新激发我的编码爱好。最初的蜜月过去后，我意识到自己陷入了困境。如果我使用人工智能“只处理”系统中我不想理解的部分，我总是会陷入这样的境地：我必须扔掉一大堆工作。但如果不了解它应该做什么，我就无法进行监督，如果我知道它应该做什么，我就可以自己做。

所以我决定进行非常渐进的工作。当我在 Neovim 的界面上工作时，将代码块剪切并粘贴到 Web 界面中是一件很烦人的事情，花了一整天的时间意识到我不能相信它可以检测 Neovim，并且不想学习足够的 lua 来管理它。 （我从 Emacs 转向 neovim，因为我不喜欢 elisp，而 gpt 在我的 emacs 设置上比 Neovim 更糟糕，最终目标是我自己的 ruby​​ 编辑器，但 gpt 肯定无法理解那个 atm）但至少我正在推动一个真正的飞轮，而不是 Fantasia 的扫帚。

I've been using ChatGPT to re-bootstrap my coding hobby. After the initial honeymoon wore off, I realized I was staring down the barrel of a dilemma. If I use AI to "just handle" the parts of the system I don't want to understand, I invariably end up in a situation where I gotta throw a whole bunch of work out. But I can't supervise without an understanding of what it's supposed to be doing, and if I knew what it was supposed to be doing, I could just do it myself.

So I settled on very incremental work. It's annoying cutting and pasting code blocks into the web interface while I'm working on my interface to Neovim, spent a whole day realizing I can't trust it to instrument neovim and don't want to learn enough lua to manage it. (I moved onto neovim from Emacs because I don't like elisp and gpt is even worse at working on my emacs setup than neovim, the end goal is my own editor in ruby but gpt damn sure can't understand that atm) But at least I'm pushing a real flywheel and not the brooms from Fantasia.

在 HN 中查看
Warwolt 2026/04/06 02:25
实际上，我认为在这种情况下 LLMS _可以_有用。如果我们提示足够小的输出，例如围绕我们已经可以推理的事物，我们就能够判断所提供的内容是否有意义。

想必您也在阅读某种有关中文的学习文本，所以唯一的来源不仅仅是法学硕士？

根据我的经验，要求法学硕士提供众所周知的事物的小例子（或者更确切地说，在培训数据中经常讨论的事物，通常是基本或基本的主题）往往效果很好，并且将达到您自己可以判断所呈现内容的水平。

我认为真正的危险是当一个人提出一些他们不知道如何自己验证的事情时，从那时起我们基本上只是掷骰子并希望

Actually, I think this is a case where LLMS _can_ be useful. If we're prompting for small enough outputs, for examples around things we can already sort of reason about it, we're able to judge whether or not what's presented to use makes sense.

Presumably you're also reading some kind of learning text about the Chinese language, so the sole source isn't just the LLM?

In my experience, asking an LLM to produce small examples of well-known things (or rather, things that are going to be talked about frequently in the training data, so generally basic or fundamental topics) tend to work fine, and is going to be at a level where you yourself can judge what's presented.

I think the real danger is when a person is prompting things they don't know how to verify for themselves, since then we're basically just rolling dice and hoping

如果您是一家大型公司，拥有应用这些知识所需的所有知识和所有技术，那么您就不需要 Alice。你不_想要_爱丽丝。你想要更多鲍勃。书上看起来更好。

故事与时间一样古老。

> Which means we need people like Alice! We have to make space for people like Alice, and find a way to promote her over Bob, even though Bob may seem to be faster.

If you are a massive company that owns all of the knowledge and all of the technology needed to apply that knowledge, then you don't need Alice. You don't _want_ Alice. You want more Bobs. It looks better on the books.

Tale as old as time.

thePhytochemist 2026/04/06 00:58
我完全同意——这篇文章以非常明显的方式忽略了这一点。这表明爱丽丝和鲍勃将以同一级别毕业。

相反，鲍勃很可能发表两篇论文。然后，他基于其他人对“出版或灭亡”的坚持，在竞争中击败了爱丽丝。爱丽丝失业了，挣扎着，被赶了出去。

投入时间和精力的人不仅仅处于同一水平，而且他们也不仅仅找到体面的工作。竞争发生，真正的学习被认为是浪费时间，这会导致现实的、常常危及生命的后果（比如找不到工作后无家可归）。

I totally agree - the article misses this point in a very conspicuous way. It suggests that Alice and Bob will both graduate at the same level.

What may well happen instead is that Bob publishes two papers. He then outcompetes Alice based on the insistence that others have on "publish or perish". Alice becomes unemployed and struggles, having been pushed out.

The person who puts the time and effort in doesn't just sit at the same level and they don't both just find decent employment. Competition happens and the authentic learning is considered a waste of time, which leads to real and often life threatening consequences (like being homeless after being unable to find employment).

在 HN 中查看
iugtmkbdfil834 2026/04/06 01:49
<<真正的学习被认为是浪费时间

我认为这可能是更有趣的一点。有趣的是，史蒂夫·乔布斯在学校里写过书法，有些人认为这是浪费时间，但史蒂夫将一些风格上的选择归功于他。

那么问题就变成它是现在还是以后会成为一个问题。看到一些输出后，我毫不怀疑非程序员现在可以构建很多东西（包括我自己；我想我属于相邻的类别）。构建模块是存在的，只要问题是初始培训的一部分，法学硕士很可能会帮助您构建您想要的东西。

它可能不完美、不安全或优化，但它可能仍然正是用户想要做的。现在，当这些产品不可避免地进入大公司生产时，问题就会开始出现。从某种意义上说，我们在过去几周看到了一些有趣的结果（包括意外的克劳德代码发布）。

从总体上看，没有什么变化……除了变化的速度。 但我们准备好了吗？

<< authentic learning is considered a waste of time

This, I think, may be the more interesting bit. Steve Jobs anecdotally did caligraphy in school, which some would consider a waste of time, but Steve credited some of the stylistic choices to.

The question then becomes whether it will become an issue now or later. Having seen some of the output, I have no doubt that a lot can now be built by non-programmers ( including myself; I suppose I belong in the adjacent category ). The building blocks exist and as long as the problem was part of the initial training, odds are, LLM will help you build what you want.

It may not be perfect, safe, or optimized, but it may still be exactly what the user wanted to do. Now, the problems will start when those will, inevitably, move into production at big corps. In a sense, we have seen some interesting results of that in the past few weeks ( including accidental claude code release ).

In a grand scheme of things, not much is changing... except for speed of change. But are we quite ready for this?

rstuart4133 2026/04/06 07:37
我自己的版本是我想要 CSV 格式的银行交易。然而，这些交易已经有一年多了，银行只以可下载的形式提供最近的交易。不过，他们确实提供了 PDF 格式的报表，可以无限期地追溯。但 PDF 中对象的排列方式使得 pdftotext 输出几乎难以辨认。

我想我应该给双子座一个机会。当我上传 18 页的 PDF 时，它抱怨输出超出了某个限制。所以我使用 pdftk 将其分成 4 页的块，这似乎有效 - 输出看起来非常好并且通过了几次抽查。但我不相信这些东西，只要我能踢它们。

有一个交易栏和一个运行余额栏，所以我快速检查了一下，看看每个新余额是否等于前一个余额加上交易。几乎总是如此。我将一些错误归因于转录错误。我错了。我最终发现这些错误只发生在我分割 PDF 的地方。在跟踪余额第一次出错的位置后，很明显它丢失了一些行，重复了其他行，并且交易和余额列未对齐。换句话说，这完全是垃圾。

那么为什么我的余额检查显示的错误这么少呢？我把这归因于知道一份好的银行对账单是什么样的。一份好的银行对账单会累积起来。因此它调整了所有余额，使其看起来像真正的银行对账单。我还注意到这些错误在后面的页面中变得更加频繁。我尝试将 PDF 拆分为单页，然后将它们一次加载到模型中。这对后面几页没有多大帮助，但第一页通常很好。然后我将每个页面加载到一个新的上下文中，并带有一个新的提示。如果这不能产生平衡的东西，那么第二次总会产生平衡。

我不确定它最终是否比手动完成节省了时间。现在这是一个令人厌倦的类比，但这是事实：从本质上讲，这些东西都是随机的鹦鹉。当给定相同的输入时，它们几乎不会两次产生相同的输出。相反，它们产生的输出很可能遵循所提供的输入标记。如果只有一个正确的输出，但输出足够小，那么他们正确的可能性就很大。但一旦规模增大，它输出完全垃圾的可能性就几乎确定了。

My own version of this is that I wanted bank transactions in CSV format. However, the transactions were more than a year old, and the bank only provides recent transactions in a downloadable form. They do, however, provide statements in PDF format going back indefinitely. But the objects in the PDF are arranged in a way that made pdftotext output near-indecipherable.

I thought I'd give Gemini a go. When I uploaded the 18-page PDF, it complained the output exceeded some limit. So I used pdftk to break it up into 4-page chunks, which seemed to work - the output looked very good and passed a couple of spot checks. But I don't trust these things as far as I can kick them.

There was a transaction column and a running balance column, so I did a quick check to see if every new balance equalled the previous one plus the transaction. And it almost always did. There were a couple of errors I put down to transcription errors. I was wrong. I eventually twigged that these errors only happened where I had split the PDFs. After tracking where the balance first went wrong, it became evident it had dropped chunks of lines, duplicated others, and misaligned the transaction and balance columns. It was complete rubbish, in other words.

So why did my balance check show so few errors? I put that down to it knowing what a good bank statement looked like. A good bank statement adds up. So it adjusted all the balances so it looked like a real bank statement. I also noticed these errors got more frequent in later pages. I tried splitting the PDF into single pages and loading them into the model one at a time. That didn't help much for the later pages, but the first one was usually good. So then I loaded each page into a fresh context, with a fresh prompt. If that didn't produce something that balanced, the second go always did.

I'm not sure it saved time over doing it manually in the end. It's a tired analogy now, but it's true: at their heart, these things are stochastic parrots. They almost never produce the same output twice when given the same input. Instead, they produce output that has a high probability of following the input tokens supplied. If there is only one correct output but the output is small enough, the odds are decent they will get it right. But once the size grows, the odds of it outputting complete crap become a near certainty.

在 HN 中查看
everdrive 2026/04/06 02:17
>所以矛盾的是，法学硕士只有在你是施瓦茨的情况下才有用†

对于这么多工人来说，他们的公司只是希望他们生产废话。他们的经理不会这样设计，但如果他们的下属开始以严格的智力严谨性来完成工作，这将成为一个问题，下属会听到它。

所以，你没有错。但大多数LLM客户并不关心，他们只想在内部报告成功，产品需要“足够好”。法学硕士可能会制作出糟糕的网页。只要页面加载，就不会注意到或关心它的错误，就像物理论文可能是错误的一样。

>And so the paradox is, the LLMs are only useful† if you're Schwartz

For so many workers, their companies just want them to produce bullshit. Their managers wouldn't frame it this way, but if their subordinates start producing work with strict intellectual rigor it's going to be an issue and the subordinates will hear about it.

So, you're not wrong. But the majority of LLM customers don't care and they just want to report success internally, and the product needs to be "just good enough." An LLM might produce a shitty webpage. So long as the page loads no on will ever notice or care that it's wrong in the way that a physics paper could be wrong.

在 HN 中查看
foobarian 2026/04/06 05:18
我愤世嫉俗地认为，20 世纪已经耗尽了推动全球发展的重大科学发现，而现在，在促进科学发展方面做得好已经不那么重要了。

过去，如果你忽视了你的研究所和学术界，会发生什么？敌对国家首先获得电力/汽车/核武器，你就会被SOL。

这些天，发生了什么？他们发明了更快的手机？更高分辨率的视频？

这种说法可能有点夸张，但我发现这是一个很好的思维练习提示。

My cynical take is that the 20th century exhausted the major scientific discoveries that move various global needles, and now doing well at fostering science matters less.

In the past, if you neglected your institutes and academia, what happened? A rival state got electricity/cars/nuclear weapons first and you would be SOL.

These days, what happens? They invent faster phones? Higher res video?

This take may be a bit hyperbolic but I find it's a good thought exercise prompt.

sd9 2026/04/05 19:42
问题是，特工不会消失。因此，如果鲍勃可以与代理一起做事，他就可以做事。

我对失去对智力刺激的编程问题的研究感到悲痛，但这是我工作的一部分正在消失。我需要决定剩下的工作——理解需求、管理团队等等——是否仍然足够有趣，可以继续下去。

老实说，我正在考虑离开软件，因为这份工作已经变得与我签约时不同。

所以我认为这篇文章部分正确，鲍勃没有学习我们过去需要的那些技能。但我认为市场将停止评估这些技能，所以这并不是一个真正的_问题_，除了鲍勃自己的智力损失。

我不喜欢这样，但我会努力去面对。

The thing is, agents aren’t going away. So if Bob can do things with agents, he can do things.

I mourn the loss of working on intellectually stimulating programming problems, but that’s a part of my job that’s fading. I need to decide if the remaining work - understanding requirements, managing teams, what have you - is still enjoyable enough to continue.

To be honest, I’m looking at leaving software because the job has turned into a different sort of thing than what I signed up for.

So I think this article is partly right, Bob is not learning those skills which we used to require. But I think the market is going to stop valuing those skills, so it’s not really a _problem_, except for Bob’s own intellectual loss.

I don’t like it, but I’m trying to face up to it.

在 HN 中查看
djaro 2026/04/05 19:51
> 因此，如果鲍勃可以与代理一起做事，他就可以做事。

当鲍勃遇到一个过于复杂或独特而代理无法解决的问题时，就会出现问题。

对我来说，这似乎有点像学习如何做饭和购买微波炉晚餐之间的区别。当然，一顿美味的微波炉晚餐味道真的很好，而且比新手厨师做的要好得多。但想象一下，有抱负的厨师只是购买预制餐点，因为“那些不会去任何地方”。经过多年的努力，最终一个真正的厨师将能够做出比你在杂货店买到的任何东西都更好的饭菜。

市场永远会看重法学硕士所能做的事情 不是 这样做，因为如果法学硕士可以做某事，就没有理由为此雇用一个人。

> So if Bob can do things with agents, he can do things.

The problem arrises when Bob encounters a problem too complex or unique for agents to solve.

To me, it seems a bit like the difference between learning how to cook versus buying microwave dinners. Sure, a good microwave dinner can taste really good, and it will be a lot better than what a beginning cook will make. But imagine aspiring cooks just buying premade meals because "those aren't going anywhere". Over the span of years, eventually a real cook will be able to make way better meals than anything you can buy at a grocery store.

The market will always value the exact things LLMs can not do, because if an LLM can do something, there is no reason to hire a person for that.

Precisely. The first 10 rungs of the ladder will be removed, but we still expect you to be able to get to the roof. The AI won't get you there and you won't have the knowledge you'd normally gain on those first 10 rungs to help you move past #10.

NiloCK 2026/04/05 21:23
在此之前，人们也会对图形计算器或计算器说同样的话。苏格拉底对书面文字也说了同样的话。

决定因素始终是“我是否想出了这个工具”。不知何故，后代总是设法找到自己的能力（公平地说，可能会有所不同）。

这不是 保证 但在我们真正看到科学、工程等前沿领域的产出大幅减少之前，这应该是默认的预期。

People would have said the same about graphing calculators or calculators before that. Socrates said the same thing about the written word.

The determining factor is always "did I come up with this tool". Somehow, subsequent generations always manage to find their own competencies (which, to be fair, may be different).

This isn't guaranteed to play out, but it should be the default expectation until we actually see greatly diminishing outputs at the frontier of science, engineering, etc.

lukev 2026/04/05 21:33
但我认为这个类比太简单了。

计算器是确定性的 正确的 给定正确的输入。不需要专家判断他们给出的答案是否合理。

作为一个整天使用法学硕士进行编码并经常突破自己能力界限的人，情况并非如此。我能够有效地使用它们的唯一原因是因为我知道好的软件是什么样子以及何时需要更明确的说明。

I think that's too easy an analogy, though.

Calculators are deterministically correct given the right input. It does not require expert judgement on whether an answer they gave is reasonable or not.

As someone who uses LLMs all day for coding, and who regularly bumps against the boundaries of what they're capable of, that's very much not the case. The only reason I can use them effectively is because I know what good software looks like and when to drop down to more explicit instructions.

在 HN 中查看
II2II 2026/04/05 22:12
> 计算器是确定性的 正确的

计算器是确定性的，但它们不一定是正确的。考虑 32 位整数算术：

  30000000 * 1000 / 1000
  30000000 / 1000 * 1000
从数学上来说，它们是相同的。从计算角度来看，结果是确定性的。另一方面，计算机会产生不同的结果。在许多其他情况下，预期结果与计算机计算的结果不同。
> Calculators are deterministically correct

Calculators are deterministic, but they are not necessarily correct. Consider 32-bit integer arithmetic:

  30000000 * 1000 / 1000
  30000000 / 1000 * 1000
Mathematically, they are identical. Computationally, the results are deterministic. On the other hand, the computer will produce different results. There are many other cases where the expected result is different from what a computer calculates.

II2II 2026/04/06 00:43
Yeap, the specific example wasn't important. I choose an example involving the order of operations and an integer overflow simply because it would be easy to discuss. (I have been out of the field for nearly 20 years now.) Your example of floating point errors is another. I also encountered artifacts from approximations for transcendental functions.

Choosing a "better" language was not always an option, at least at the time. I was working with grad students who were managing huge datasets, sometimes for large simulations and sometimes from large surveys. They were using C. Some of the faculty may have used Fortran. C exposes you the vulgarities of the hardware, and I'm fairly certain Fortran does as well. They weren't going to use a calculator for those tasks, nor an interpreted language. Even if they wanted to choose another language, the choice of languages was limited by the machines they used. I've long since forgotten what the high performance cluster was running, but it wasn't Linux and it wasn't on Intel. They may have been able to license something like Mathematica for it, but that wasn't the type of computation they were doing.

yunwal 2026/04/05 21:56
决定论只是意味着您不必使用统计数据来得出正确的答案。这并不是什么灵丹妙药，可以神奇地让事情变得容易理解，而且如果系统中缺少它，你就不可能理解它，这也是不正确的。
Determinism just means you don't have to use statistics to approach the right answer. It's not some silver bullet that magically makes things understandable and it's not true that if it's missing from a system you can't possibly understand it.

lukev 2026/04/05 22:07
That's not what I mean.

If I use a calculator to find a logarithm, and I know what a logarithm is, then the answer the calculator gives me is perfectly useful and 100% substitutable for what I would have found if I'd calculated the logarithm myself.

If I use Claude to "build a login page", it will definitely build me a login page. But there's a very real chance that what it generated contains a security issue. If I'm an experienced engineer I can take a quick look and validate whether it does or whether it doesn't, but if I'm not, I've introduced real risk to my application.

That's not what I mean.

If I use a calculator to find a logarithm, and I know what a logarithm is, then the answer the calculator gives me is perfectly useful and 100% substitutable for what I would have found if I'd calculated the logarithm myself.

If I use Claude to "build a login page", it will definitely build me a login page. But there's a very real chance that what it generated contains a security issue. If I'm an experienced engineer I can take a quick look and validate whether it does or whether it doesn't, but if I'm not, I've introduced real risk to my application.

在 HN 中查看
threatofrain 2026/04/05 22:19
Those two tasks are just very different. In one world you have provided a complete specification, such as 1 + 1, for which the calculator responds with some answer and both you and the machine have a decidable procedure for judging answers. In another world you have engaged in a declaration for which the are many right and wrong answers, and thus even the boundaries of error are in question.

It's equivalent to asking your friend to pick you up, and they arrive in a big vs small car. Maybe you needed a big car because you were going to move furniture, or maybe you don't care, oops either way.

Those two tasks are just very different. In another world you have engaged in a declaration for which the are many right and wrong answers, and thus even the boundaries of error are in question.

It's equivalent to asking your friend to pick you up, and they arrive in a big vs small car. Maybe you needed a big car because you were going to move furniture, or maybe you don't care, oops either way.

bsder 2026/04/06 07:38
> Calculators are deterministically correct given the right input. It does not require expert judgement on whether an answer they gave is reasonable or not.

That's not actually true. The HP-12C calculator is still the dominant calculator in business schools 45 years later precisely because it did take expert judgement to determine whether certain interest and amortization calculations were reasonable.

> Calculators are deterministically correct given the right input. It does not require expert judgement on whether an answer they gave is reasonable or not.

That's not actually true. The HP-12C calculator is still the dominant calculator in business schools 45 years later precisely because it did take expert judgement to determine whether certain interest and amortization calculations were reasonable.

在 HN 中查看
didgetmaster 2026/04/05 22:10
如果你把一个坏掉的计算器交给一个懂数学的人，他们输入 123 + 765，结果是 6789；他们应该立即知道出了问题。将计算器交给一个从来不理解该工具实际用途而只是接受出现的任何答案的人；他们可能会认为答案是完全合理的。

发现法学硕士的幻觉通常需要在提出问题之前对答案应该是什么样子有一个基本的了解。

If you hand a broken calculator to someone who knows how to do math, and they entered 123 + 765 which produced an answer of 6789; they should instantly know something is wrong. Hand that calculator to someone who never understood what the tool actually did but just accepted whatever answer appeared; and they would likely think the answer was totally reasonable.

Catching an LLM hallucinating often takes a basic understanding of what the answer should look like before asking the question.

在 HN 中查看
abustamam 2026/04/06 00:21
One time when I was a kid I was playing with my older sister's graphing calculator. I had accidentally pressed the base button and now was in hex mode. I did some benign calculation like 10+10 and got 14. I believed it!

I went to school the next day and told my teacher that the calculator says that 10+10 is 14, so why does she say it's 20?

So she showed me on her calculator. She pressed the hex button and explained why it was 14.

I think a major problem with people's usage of LLMs is that they stop at 10+10=14. They don't question it or ask someone (even the LLM) to explain the answer.

I went to school the next day and told my teacher that the calculator says that 10+10 is 14, so why does she say it's 20?

So she showed me on her calculator. She pressed the hex button and explained why it was 14.

I think a major problem with people's usage of LLMs is that they stop at 10+10=14. They don't question it or ask someone (even the LLM) to explain the answer.

abustamam 2026/04/06 05:41

在 HN 中查看
MarkusQ 2026/04/06 03:51
> Catching an LLM hallucinating often takes a basic understanding of what the answer should look like before asking the question.

We had the same problem in the early days of calculators. Using a slide rule, you had to track the order of magnitude in your head; this habit let you spot a large class of errors (things that weren't even close to correct).

When calculators came on the scene, people who never used a slide rule would confidently accept answers that were wildly incorrect (example: a mole of ideal gas at STP is 22.4 liters. If you typo it as 2204, you get an answer that's off by roughly two orders of magnitude, say 0.0454 when it should be 4.46. Easy to spot if you know roughly what the answer should look like, but easy to miss if you don't).

> Catching an LLM hallucinating often takes a basic understanding of what the answer should look like before asking the question.

We had the same problem in the early days of calculators. Using a slide rule, you had to track the order of magnitude in your head; this habit let you spot a large class of errors (things that weren't even close to correct).

When calculators came on the scene, people who never used a slide rule would confidently accept answers that were wildly incorrect (example: a mole of ideal gas at STP is 22.4 liters. If you typo it as 2204, you get an answer that's off by roughly two orders of magnitude, say 0.0454 when it should be 4.46. Easy to spot if you know roughly what the answer should look like, but easy to miss if you don't).

nothrabannosir 2026/04/05 22:49
当人们提到“苏格拉底谈论写作”时，他们到底在指什么？斐德罗？

> “最聪明的泰特，一个人有能力创造艺术，但判断艺术对其使用者有用或有害的能力属于另一个人；[275a]而现在，你，作为文学之父，在你的感情的引导下，赋予了艺术一种与他们真正拥有的力量相反的力量。

> “因为这项发明会在那些学习使用它的人的头脑中产生健忘，因为他们不会练习他们的记忆。他们对由不属于他们自己的外部字符产生的书写的信任，会阻止他们使用他们自己的记忆。你发明了一种不是记忆的灵丹妙药，而是提醒的灵丹妙药；你为你的学生提供了智慧的外表，而不是真正的智慧，因为他们会在没有指导的情况下阅读许多东西，因此似乎[275b]知道很多事情，而当他们在大多数情况下无知且难以相处，因为他们并不聪明，而只是显得聪明。”

在我看来，他是对的。

What do people mean exactly when they bring up “Socrates saying things about writing”? Phaedrus?

> “Most ingenious Theuth, one man has the ability to beget arts, but the ability to judge of their usefulness or harmfulness to their users belongs to another; [275a] and now you, who are the father of letters, have been led by your affection to ascribe to them a power the opposite of that which they really possess.

> "For this invention will produce forgetfulness in the minds of those who learn to use it, because they will not practice their memory. Their trust in writing, produced by external characters which are no part of themselves, will discourage the use of their own memory within them. You have invented an elixir not of memory, but of reminding; and you offer your pupils the appearance of wisdom, not true wisdom, for they will read many things without instruction and will therefore seem [275b] to know many things, when they are for the most part ignorant and hard to get along with, since they are not wise, but only appear wise."

Sounds to me like he was spot on.

ThrowawayR2 2026/04/05 22:41
由于同样的原因，计算器的类比也是错误的。 了解并内化算术、代数和曲线的形状等是进入高等数学并成为数学家或物理学家的数学阶梯。 你无法在没有理解的情况下使用计算器即插即用。

进行计算器类比的人已经是缺失梯级问题的受害者，他们甚至无法理解自己缺少什么。 这就是法学硕士过度使用的未来将带我们去的地方。

The calculator analogy is wrong for the same reason. Knowing and internalizing arithmetic, algebra, and the shape of curves, etc. are mathematical rungs to get to higher mathematics and becoming a mathematician or physicist. You can't plug-and-chug your way there with a calculator and no understanding.

The people who make the calculator analogy are already victims of the missing rung problem and they aren't even able to comprehend what they're lacking. That's where the future of LLM overuse will take us.

在 HN 中查看
Jensson 2026/04/05 21:55
> 在此之前，人们也会对图形计算器或计算器说同样的话。苏格拉底对书面文字也说了同样的话。

嗯，我们仍然让人们手动计算很多年，我们仍然让人们听讲座而不仅仅是阅读。

但是我们还会有人经历多年的手动编码吗？我想将来我们会强迫他们，至少如果我们想让人们保持能力的话，就像你提到的其他事情一样。目前你在工作中这样做，将来人们不会在工作中这样做，因此他们将被期望将其作为教育的一部分。

> People would have said the same about graphing calculators or calculators before that. Socrates said the same thing about the written word.

Well, we still make people calculate manually for many years, and we still make people listen to lectures instead of just reading.

But will we still have people to go through years of manual coding? I guess in the future we will force them, at least if we want to keep people competent, just like the other things you mentioned. Currently you do that on the job, in the future people wont do that on the job so they will be expected to do it as a part of their education.

在 HN 中查看
II2II 2026/04/05 21:59
> 决定因素始终是“我是否想出了这个工具”。不知何故，后代总是设法找到自己的能力（公平地说，可能会有所不同）。

从某种意义上说，我认为你是对的。我们目前正在经历一个重视某些技能而贬低其他技能的过渡时期。那些因为不必做无意义的繁重工作而看到生产力大幅提高的人们对此充满热情。没有发明这个工具的人很快就会指出其中的陷阱。

事实是，反对者并没有错，因为我们选择遵循的道路将决定使用该技术的结果。用它来筛选论文以找出值得深入阅读的内容是很有用的。用它来帮助我们理解论文中的难点是很有用的。另一方面，用它来代替阅读论文会适得其反。它正在用机器“认为”作者所说的内容取代作者所说的内容。这可能会消除不必要的冗长，但几乎肯定也会消除必要的细节。

我的大学时光是用来学习天体物理学的。那是很久以前的事了，但处理数据的技术的斗争是相似的。老教师之间存在着争论，老教师对计算机很熟悉，只要研究人员在场监督分析的每一步，而新教师则需要计算机在没有人工干预的情况下获取原始数据以减少结果。一如既往，原因是生产力。如果人们必须干预其中的任何一步，他们就无法处理新一代传感器或系统性大规模调查生成的大量数据。在基本层面上，您无法弄清楚这是否是垃圾输入、垃圾输出类型的场景，因为没有人有时间查看输入。 （我的意思是绝对意义上没有时间。数据太多了。）在更深的层次上，你甚至无法判断数据处理步骤是否有效，除非数据有明显的错误。当然，代码看起来不错。如果代码符合我们的预期，从数学上来说，那就没问题了。但有时我不得不指出计算机没有按照他们想象的方式工作。

这是一场双方都对的辩论。如果不让计算机介入，也不让计算机接管繁重的工作，你就无法以有效的速度取得科学进步。另一方面，机器不能替代繁重的理解工作，可能涉及从计算机科学家（而不是数学家）的角度阅读论文或分析代码。

> The determining factor is always "did I come up with this tool". Somehow, subsequent generations always manage to find their own competencies (which, to be fair, may be different).

In a sense, I think you are right. We are currently going through a period of transition that values some skills and devalues others. The people who see huge productivity gains because they don't have to do the meaningless grunt work are enthusiastic about that. The people who did not come up with the tool are quick to point out pitfalls.

The thing is, the naysayers aren't wrong since the path we choose to follow will determine the outcome of using the technology. Using it to sift through papers to figure out what is worth reading in depth is useful. Using it to help us understand difficult points in a paper is useful. On the other hand, using it as a replacement for reading the papers is counterproductive. It is replacing what the author said with what a machine "thinks" an author said. That may get rid of unnecessary verbosity, but it is almost certainly stripping away necessary details as well.

My university days were spent studying astrophysics. It was long ago, but the struggles with technology handling data were similar. There were debates between older faculty who were fine with computers, as long as researchers were there to supervise the analysis every step of the way, and new faculty, who needed computers to take raw data to reduced results without human intervention. The reason was, as always, productivity. People could not handle the massive amounts of data being generated by the new generation of sensors or systematic large scale surveys if they had to intervene any step of the way. At a basic level, you couldn't figure out whether it was a garbage-in, garbage-out type scenario because no one had the time to look at the inputs. (I mean no time in an absolute sense. There was too much data.) At a deeper level, you couldn't even tell if the data processing steps were valid unless there was something obviously wrong with the data. Sure, the code looked fine. If the code did what we expected of it, mathematically, it would be fine. But there were occasions where I had to point out that the computer isn't working how they thought it was.

It was a debate in which both sides were right. You couldn't make scientific progress at a useful pace without sticking computers in the middle and without computers taking over the grunt work. On the other hand, the machine cannot be used as a replacement for the grunt work of understanding, may that involves reading papers or analyzing the code from the perspective of a computer scientist (rather than a mathematician).

我们仍然希望高中生在使用 TI-83 之前先学会使用方格纸，小学生在使用计算器之前先学会手工算术。这本质上是这篇文章的要点，只有当你学会了如何在没有法学硕士的情况下完成工作之后，法学硕士才是一个有用的工具。
We still expect high school students to learn to use graph paper before they use their TI-83, grade school students to do arithmetic by hand before using a calculator. This is essentially the post's point, that LLMs are a useful tool only after you have learned to do the work without them.

beepbooptheory 2026/04/05 21:50
苏格拉底并没有对书面文字这么说。柏拉图让苏格拉底在《斐德罗篇》的开头部分谈到写作，但这不是苏格拉底的观点，也不是他得出的最终结论。

是的，你可以调出报价或询问你的人工智能，但他们会错的。这句话出自苏格拉底背诵的“神话”，这在这样的中后期对话中非常典型。

但在这里，唉，我们可以认识到完全的荒谬，这只是指出了为什么写作 能 做坏事，就像苏格拉底所做的那样。因为你让 2000 年后的人们利用你并错误引用你的观点来实现他们愚蠢的目标！不再 标志， 只有无限的随机 多克斯。真正是智者的未来！

Socrates does not say this about the written word. Plato has Socrates say it about writing in the beginning sections of the Phaedrus, but it is not Socrates opinion nor the final conclusion he arrives at.

And yes yes you can pull up the quote or ask your AI, but they will be wrong. The quote is from Socrates reciting a "myth", as is pretty typical in a middle late dialogue like this.

But here, alas we can recognize the utter absurdity, that this just points out why writing can be bad, as Socrates does pose. Because you get guys 2000 years in future using you and misquoting you for their dumb cause! No more logos, only endless stochastic doxa. Truly a future of sophists!

cweld510 2026/04/06 03:02
AI is only eating some of that though. For instance, everyone who does performance work knows that perhaps the most important part of optimization is constructing the right benchmark. This is already the thing that makes intractable problems tractable. That effect is now exacerbated — AI can optimize anything given a benchmark —- but AI isn’t making great progress at constructing the benchmark itself.
AI is only eating some of that though. For instance, everyone who does performance work knows that perhaps the most important part of optimization is constructing the right benchmark. This is already the thing that makes intractable problems tractable. That effect is now exacerbated — AI can optimize anything given a benchmark —- but AI isn’t making great progress at constructing the benchmark itself.

在 HN 中查看
skippyboxedhero 2026/04/05 22:12
正确的区别是：如果没有代理你做不到某件事，那么你就做不到。

作者所描述的问题是真实存在的。我现在已经遇到过数百次了。我会知道如何做某事，我告诉人工智能去做，人工智能实际上并不知道如何从根本上做这件事，并且会创建假测试来证明它已经完成，然后你检查工作，发现它是错误的。

你可以向人工智能描述在非常高的水平上做某事，但如果你不知道如何检查结果，那么人工智能就没有用处。

关于厨师的故事是100%正确的。麦当劳没有“厨师”，他们有组装食物的工厂工人。人工智能的论点是，在麦当劳工作意味着你能够像最好的厨师一样烹饪食物。

招聘的问题在于，公司将无法区分人工智能驱动的人类和有知识的人，直到为时已晚。

如果你有知识并且正确使用人工智能工具（即不尝试零样本工作），那么它是一个巨大的乘数。该行业正在转向代理驱动的工作流程，这表明人工智能业务就是向无能的人出售虚假的专业知识。

The correct distinction is: if you can't do something without the agent, then you can't do it.

The problem that the author describes is real. I have run into it hundreds of times now. I will know how to do something, I tell AI to do it, the AI does not actually know how to do it at a fundamental level and will create fake tests to prove that it is done, and you check the work and it is wrong.

You can describe to the AI to do X at a very high-level but if you don't know how to check the outcome then the AI isn't going to be useful.

The story about the cook is 100% right. McDonald's doesn't have "chefs", they have factory workers who assemble food. The argument with AI is that working in McDonald's means you are able to cook food as well as the best chef.

The issue with hiring is that companies won't be able to distinguish between AI-driven humans and people with knowledge until it is too late.

If you have knowledge and are using AI tools correctly (i.e. not trying to zero-shot work) then it is a huge multiplier. That the industry is moving towards agent-driven workflows indicates that the AI business is about selling fake expertise to the incompetent.

在 HN 中查看
klabb3 2026/04/05 23:38
> 当 Bob 遇到过于复杂或独特而代理无法解决的问题时，就会出现问题。

实际上比这更糟糕：人工智能不会停下来并说“太复杂了，一个月后尝试下一个 SOTA 模型”。相反，它会给鲍勃提供一个看似合理的解决方案，但鲍勃无法识别正确或错误。如果鲍勃正在处理即时反馈问题，那没关系：他可以标记该问题，重试，寻求帮助。但如果错误不能立即被发现，一年后它可能会再次出现。也许那时鲍勃已经升职了，鲍勃的继任者就可以处理这个问题。无论哪种情况，鲍勃都不能比法学硕士本身更值得信任。

> The problem arrises when Bob encounters a problem too complex or unique for agents to solve.

It’s actually worse than that: the AI will not stop and say ”too complex, try in a month with the next SOTA model”. Rather, it will give Bob a plausible looking solution that Bob cannot identify as right or wrong. If Bob is working on an instant feedback problem, it’s ok: he can flag it, try again, ask for help. But if the error can’t be detected immediately, it can come back with a vengeance in a year. Perhaps Bob has already gotten promoted by then, and Bobs replacement gets to deal with it. In either case, Bob cannot be trusted any more than the LLM itself.

在 HN 中查看
noisy_boy 2026/04/06 10:10
鲍勃的问题是怎么回事？

当他说我们需要更多时间来正确完成这项工作时，他被贴上了“缓慢”的标签。他们整天催促他使用人工智能，并告诉所有人，会有程序员使用人工智能，而那些不使用人工智能的程序员将被抛在后面。所以他说他妈的为项目做正确的事，让我为自己做正确的事。

现在他得到了晋升，他们将在更便宜的地点雇用 3 个人来处理即将出现的各种问题（产品总会有你看到的错误）。鉴于他出色的交付速度，他们将向他汇报。

对鲍勃有好处。

How is that Bob's problem?

When he said we need more time to do this properly, he was labelled slow. They pushed him to use AI all day long and told at the all hands that there will be programmers who use AI and those who don't will be left behind. So he said fuck doing it right for the project, let me do it right for myself.

Now he got his promotion, they will hire 3 people in a cheaper location to handle various issues that are coming up (product will always have bugs you see). Given his excellent speed of delivery, they will report to him.

Good for Bob.

在 HN 中查看
klabb3 2026/04/06 11:08
> 鲍勃的问题是怎么回事？

事实并非如此。鲍勃有一个不同的问题：有数百万鲍勃可以使用相同的工具，这意味着鲍勃的劳动力价值是商品定价的。这可能对某些鲍勃有利，但对其他人不利。

> How is that Bob's problem?

It isn’t. Bob has a different problem: that there are millions of Bobs with access to the same tools, meaning the value of Bobs labor is commodity priced. That may be good for some Bobs and bad for others.

To me it feels more like learning to cook versus learning how to repair ovens and run a farm. Software engineering isn’t about writing code any more than it’s about writing machine code or designing CPUs. It’s about bringing great software into existence.

roenxi 2026/04/05 20:20
这听起来不是什么大问题。鲍勃已经遇到了太大、太复杂的问题，无论是代理还是其他方式，他都无法解决。生活给我们带来了难题。我不记得我们是否认为鲍勃异常有能力，他可能是生活中的挣扎者之一。我很有可能认为，如果他在经纪人的帮助下完成了一个计划，他将在一生中至少取得正常水平的成功。

但还有一个更微妙的事情，那就是我们正趋向于通过这些人工智能实现超级智能。到那时，鲍勃可能会发现特工不能做的事情，爱丽丝也不能做，因为她试图使用湿肉而不是高性能的工程思维系统进行思考，从而受到限制。从长远来看，不会赢得这场战斗。

> 市场总是会重视法学硕士不能做的事情，因为如果法学硕士可以做某事，就没有理由为此雇用一个人。

市场看重推土机。人类是否从事实际工作对于市场来说并不是特别令人兴奋。

That doesn't sound like much of an issue. Bob was already going to encounter problems that are too large and complex for him to solve, agents or otherwise. Life throws us hard problems. I don't recall if we even assumed Bob was unusually capable, he might be one of life's flounderers. I'd give good odds that if he got through a program with the help of agents he'll get through life achieving at least a normal level of success.

But there is also a more subtle thing, which is we're trending towards superintelligence with these AIs. At the point, Bob may discover that anything agents can't do, Alice can't do because she is limited by trying to think using soggy meat as opposed to a high-performance engineered thinking system. Not going to win that battle in the long term.

> The market will always value the exact things LLMs can not do, because if an LLM can do something, there is no reason to hire a person for that.

The market values bulldozers. Whether a human does actual work or not isn't particularly exciting to a market.

在 HN 中查看
kelnos 2026/04/05 20:50
> 我们正在通过这些人工智能走向超级智能

这篇文章解决了这个问题，因为，嗯……不，我们不是。也许我们是。但目前还不清楚我们是否会在这些代理的作用上迈向平台期。

> 人类是否从事实际工作对于市场来说并不是特别令人兴奋。

你似乎相信这些人工智能代理将继续无限制地改进，所以我认为这就是脱节所在。我们中的一些人（包括文章作者）更加持怀疑态度。市场价值的工作实际上已经完成。如果人工智能有限制，而驾驶它们的人类不再有能力超越这些限制，那么那些经过艰苦学习而无需如此依赖人工智能的人将在市场上占据优势。

作为一名软件开发人员，我已经发现自己变得越来越懒，让法学硕士验证我的工作，而不是经历自己真正思考的过程。我能感觉到我的这部分技能正在萎缩。现在考虑一下那些从未发展过这些技能的人，因为法学硕士已经为他们做到了。如果 LLM 做得不好会发生什么？他们将不知道。至少我仍然这样做。

也许有一天人工智能会变得如此强大，以至于一切都不再重要。他们会比该领域最有经验的人更聪明、更全面，能够做得更多，而且做得正确。但我认为这还远未确定。

> we're trending towards superintelligence with these AIs

The article addresses this, because, well... no we aren't. Maybe we are. But it's far from clear that we're not moving toward a plateau in what these agents can do.

> Whether a human does actual work or not isn't particularly exciting to a market.

You seem to be convinced these AI agents will continue to improve without bound, so I think this is where the disconnect lies. Some of us (including the article author) are more skeptical. The market values work actually getting done. If the AIs have limits, and the humans driving them no longer have the capability to surpass those limits on their own, then people who have learned the hard way, without relying so much on an AI, will have an advantage in the market.

I already find myself getting lazy as a software developer, having an LLM verify my work, rather than going through the process of really thinking it through myself. I can feel that part of my skills atrophying. Now consider someone who has never developed those skills in the first place, because the LLM has done it for them. What happens when the LLM does a bad job of it? They'll have no idea. I still do, at least.

Maybe someday the AIs will be so capable that it won't matter. They'll be smarter and more through and be able to do more, and do it correctly, than even the most experienced person in the field. But I don't think that's even close to a certainty.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/05 21:30
超级智能没有很好的定义。计算器在进行简单的数学运算方面已经比任何人类都要强大，甚至本地使用的小型人工智能也可以立即回忆起几乎任何研究领域的各种令人印象深刻的知识，这对任何人类来说都是不可行的；但当人们想知道未来的人工智能是否会拥有超级智能时，这些都不是他们的意思。
There's no good definition of superintelligence. A calculator is already way more capable than any human at doing simple mathematical operations, and even small AIs for local use can instantly recall all sorts of impressive knowledge about virtually any field of study, which would be unfeasible for any human; but neither of those is what people mean when they wonder whether future AIs will have superintelligence.
在 HN 中查看
Jensson 2026/04/05 21:59
General superintelligence is more well defined, I assume that is what he meant. When I hear superintelligence I assume they just mean general superintelligence as in its better than humans at every single mental task that exists.
General superintelligence is more well defined, I assume that is what he meant. When I hear superintelligence I assume they just mean general superintelligence as in its better than humans at every single mental task that exists.
在 HN 中查看
dryarzeg 2026/04/05 21:08
> 但目前还不清楚我们是否会在这些代理的作用上迈向平台期。

这是一个有争议的话题，我同意你的观点，目前还不清楚我们是否会在某个时刻碰壁。但我想提的一点是，当人工智能代理刚刚被构想出来并且最流行的“人工智能”类型是基于 LLM 的聊天机器人时，我们似乎也正在接近其性能的某种平台期。然后“特工”出现了，这个高原，我们可能在某个时刻撞到的墙，边界被推得更远。我不知道（谁知道呢？）我们能把界限推到多远，但谁知道接下来会发生什么？例如，谁知道什么时候会出现一种不同于 Transformer 的全新架构并被各地采用，从而带来新的东西？未来是不确定的。今年我们可能会碰壁，或者未来10-20年我们可能不会碰壁。确实，这还不清楚。

> But it's far from clear that we're not moving toward a plateau in what these agents can do.

It is a debatable topic, and I agree with you that it's unclear whether we will hit the wall or not at some point. But one point I want to mention is that at the time when the AI agents were only conceived and the most popular type of """AI""" was LLM-based chatbot, it also seemed that we're approaching some kind of plateau in their performance. Then "agents" appeared, and this plateau, the wall we're likely to hit at some point, the boundary was pushed further. I don't know (who knows at all?) how far away we can push the boundaries, but who knows what comes next? Who knows, for example, when a completely new architecture different from Transformers will come out and be adopted everywhere, which will allow for something new? Future is uncertain. We may hit the wall this year, or we may not hit it in the next 10-20 years. It is, indeed, unclear.

dandellion 2026/04/05 21:02
> 我们正在通过这些人工智能走向超级智能

我不会指望这一点，因为即使它发生了，我们也不知道它什么时候会发生，而且它看起来有多接近并不表明它实际上有多接近。我们可以轻松地度过未来 100 年远离通用人工智能的 10 年。看看聚变发电、自动驾驶汽车等就知道了。

> we're trending towards superintelligence with these AIs

I wouldn't count on that because even if it happens, we don't know when it ill happen, and it's one of those things where how close it looks to be is no indication of how close it actually is. We could just as easily spend the next 100 years being 10 years away from agi. Just look at fusion power, self driving cars, etc.

在 HN 中查看
CuriouslyC 2026/04/05 21:07
融合并不是一个很好的例子。自动驾驶汽车是一场监管与 9 级可靠性之间的较量，如果我们愿意接受像人类一样发生车祸的自动驾驶汽车，那么它已经出现了。

无论模型有什么问题，我们都可以投入资金让它们做得更好。它非常干燥。奇怪的是，这如何有助于“通用智能”，以及模型是否会由于我们的改变而朝着全面自治的方向发展。不过，这对于 AGI 口呼吸者来说最重要，从事实际工作的人们只关心模型的改进。

Fusion isn't a good example. Self driving cars are a battle between regulation and 9's of reliability, if we were willing to accept self driving cars that crashed as much as humans it'd be here already.

Whatever models suck at, we can pour money into making them do better. It's very cut and dry. The squirrely bit is how that contributes to "general intelligence" and whether the models are progressing towards overall autonomy due to our changes. That mostly matters for the AGI mouthbreathers though, people doing actual work just care that the models have improved.

在 HN 中查看
b00ty4breakfast 2026/04/05 22:43
we have to look at what LLMs are and are not doing for this to be applicable; they are not "thinking", there is no real cognition going on inside an LLM. They are making statistical connections between data points in their training sets. Obviously, that has born some pretty interesting (and sometimes even useful) results but they are not doing anything that any reasonably informed person would call "intelligent" and certainly not "super intelligent".

在 HN 中查看
lelanthran 2026/04/05 21:10
> 鲍勃是否正常并不重要。付费让他参加这个项目是没有意义的。

是的，我对阅读这篇文章并得出结论的人感到惊讶，即该程序旨在搅动可交付成果，然后他们得出结论，如果鲍勃只能在人工智能握住他的手的情况下工作并不重要，因为他仍然可以交付。

这不是程序的输出；而是程序的输出。输出是一个爱丽丝。这就是该计划的重点。他们想要的不是Alice产生的结果，他们想要的是最终的Alice。

> It doesn't matter if Bob can be normal. There was no point to him being paid to be on the program.

Yeah, I'm surprised at the number of people who read the article and came away with the conclusion that the program was designed to churn deliverables, and then they conclude that it doesn't matter if Bob can only function with an AI holding his hand, because he can still deliver.

That isn't the output of the program; the output is an Alice. That's the point of the program. They don't want the results generated by Alice, they want the final Alice.

wizzwizz4 2026/04/05 20:49
来自文章：

> 对此有一个常见的反驳，我经常听到这样的反驳。 “等等，”人们说。 “几个月后，一年后，模型会更好。他们不会产生幻觉。他们不会伪造情节。你所描述的问题是暂时的。”自 2023 年以来，我一直听到“等等”。

是 不是 这些人工智能正在走向超级智能。我们正在走向（事实上，已经达到）超级智能 一般计算机，但 LLM 代理对于我们让它们完成的大多数任务来说是能力最差的已知算法之一。问题通常是，大多数人无法获得不起眼的研究项目的成果。

未经训练的孩子比最复杂的法学硕士写出更好的代码，甚至没有注意到他们在做任何特别的事情。

From the article:

> There's a common rebuttal to this, and I hear it constantly. "Just wait," people say. "In a few months, in a year, the models will be better. They won't hallucinate. They won't fake plots. The problems you're describing are temporary." I've been hearing "just wait" since 2023.

We're not trending towards superintelligence with these AIs. We're trending towards (and, in fact, have already reached) superintelligence with computers in general, but LLM agents are among the least capable known algorithms for the majority of tasks we get them to do. The problem, as it usually is, is that most people don't have access to the fruits of obscure research projects.

Untrained children write better code than the most sophisticated LLMs, without even noticing they're doing anything special.

在 HN 中查看
wizzwizz4 2026/04/05 23:11
LLMs are still making fundamentally the same kinds of errors that they made in 2021. If you check my HN comment history, you'll see I predicted these errors, just from skimming the relevant academic papers (which is to say they're obvious: I'm far from the only person saying this). There is no theoretical reason we should expect them to go away, unless the model architectures fundamentally change (and no, GPT -> LLaMA is not a fundamental change), because they're not removable discontinuities: they're indicative of fundamental capability gaps.

I don't care how many terms you add to your Taylor series: your polynomial approximation of a sine wave is never going to be suitable for additive speech synthesis. Likewise, I don't care how good your predictive-text transformer model gets at instrumental NLP subtasks: it will never be a good programmer (except as far as it's a plagiarist). Just look at the Claude Code source code: if anyone's an expert in agentic AI development, it's the Claude people, and yet the codebase is utterly unmaintainable dogshit that shouldn't work and, on further inspection, doesn't work.

That's not to say that no computer program can write computer programs, but this computer program is well into the realm of diminishing returns.

LLMs are still making fundamentally the same kinds of errors that they made in 2021. If you check my HN comment history, you'll see I predicted these errors, just from skimming the relevant academic papers (which is to say they're obvious: I'm far from the only person saying this). There is no theoretical reason we should expect them to go away, unless the model architectures fundamentally change (and no, GPT -> LLaMA is not a fundamental change), because they're not removable discontinuities: they're indicative of fundamental capability gaps.

I don't care how many terms you add to your Taylor series: your polynomial approximation of a sine wave is never going to be suitable for additive speech synthesis. Likewise, I don't care how good your predictive-text transformer model gets at instrumental NLP subtasks: it will never be a good programmer (except as far as it's a plagiarist). Just look at the Claude Code source code: if anyone's an expert in agentic AI development, it's the Claude people, and yet the codebase is utterly unmaintainable dogshit that shouldn't work and, on further inspection, doesn't work.

That's not to say that no computer program can write computer programs, but this computer program is well into the realm of diminishing returns.

latexr 2026/04/06 05:11
> 当 Bob 遇到过于复杂或独特而代理无法解决的问题时，就会出现问题。

或者甚至更早，当鲍勃的互联网连接中断，或者他用完代币，或者被禁止使用他最喜欢的服务，或者服务关闭，或者他需要解决无法运行本地模型的机器的问题，或者本质上是他无法使用法学硕士的任何情况。

jnovek 2026/04/05 22:00
有多少专业做饭的人是美食厨师？我认为最终，美食烹饪的需求如此之低，以至于我们不需要每个制作食物的人都这样做，只需要一小群经过专业培训的人即可。大多数以做饭为生的人都在麦当劳和 Applebee 等不需要高水平技能的地方工作。

未来仍然会有编程专家——毕竟我们仍然有装配专家和COBOL专家。我们只是不需要太多，而且绝大多数软件工程师都会使用更高级别的工具。

How many people who cook professionally are gourmet chefs? I think it ends up that gourmet cooking is so infrequently needed that we don’t require everyone who makes food to do it, just a small group of professionally trained people. Most people who make food for a living work somewhere like McDonald’s and Applebee’s where a high level of skill is not required.

There will still be programming specialists in the future — we still have assembly experts and COBOL experts, after all. We just won’t need very many of them and the vast majority of software engineers will use higher-level tools.

ThrowawayR2 2026/04/05 23:21
但这就是问题所在：成为相当于麦当劳工人的程序员将像麦当劳工人一样得到很低的报酬，并像麦当劳工人一样被视为一次性的。
That's the problem though: programmers who become the equivalent of McDonald's workers will be paid poorly like McDonald's workers and be treated as disposable like McDonald's workers.

在 HN 中查看
cfloyd 2026/04/05 21:41
我持有这种观点有一段时间，但我得出（可能是天真的）结论：这只是被迫的自信。事实上，产出不达标的问题只是一种激励和监督的缺陷。如果正确监督和推广，代理团队可以生产出更好的最终产品。问题是大多数人不会花时间这样做。我并不是说我喜欢这是事实，恰恰相反。这就是现在的现实。
I held this point of view for a while but I came to the (possibly naive) conclusion that it was just forced self-assurance. Truth is, the issues with sub-par output are just a prompting and supervision deficiency. An agent team can produce better end product if supervised and promoted correctly. The issue is most don’t take the time to do that. I’m not saying I like that this is true, quite the opposite. It is the reality of things now.

vrganj 2026/04/05 21:42
在某些时候，聚集白痴专家变得比一开始就自己做这些该死的事情还要麻烦。
At some point the herding of idiot savants becomes more work than just doing the damn thing yourself in the first place.
在 HN 中查看
lxgr 2026/04/05 22:20
我很高兴一生都在追赶白痴 如果他们走出困境时比进入时更聪明。目前法学硕士代理人的真正悲剧在于，他们实际上是无国籍的，因此所有“教育”他们的努力都感觉浪费了。

一旦持续学习得到解决，我预测 TFA 解决的问题将变得更大：如果法学硕士可以更快地学习它，将永远为你工作，并且不会请病假或考虑改变职业，那么任何人教一个人的动机是什么？

I'm happy to herd idiots all my life if they come out of it smarter than they went in. The real tragedy with current LLM agents is that they're effectively stateless, and so all the effort of "educating" them feels wasted.

Once continuous learning is solved, I predict the problem addressed by TFA to become orders of magnitude bigger: What's the motivation for anyone to teach a person if an LLM can learn it much faster, will work for you forever, and won't take any sick days or consider changing careers?
